coredata.dc:title: Facile and cost-effective preparation of carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid detection in living cells based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence principle
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xuexia
Zhou, Xi
Ma, Yufeng
Qian, Tao
Wang, Chunpeng
Chu, Fuxiang
coredata.dc:description: A facile and cost-effective route of fabricating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was proposed via simply hydrothermal treatment of lignin. Because of abundant aromatic structure and functional groups derived from lignin, the prepared CQDs (LCQDs) showed unique chemical and optical property. A remarkable fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on LCQDs was found, resulting from the formation of non-fluorescence complex between LCQDs and Fe3+. This “on-off” fluorescence CQDs nanoprobe for Fe3+ allowed a wide linear range of 50–650 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 196 nM. Besides, ascorbic acid (AA) was found to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, leading to the recovery of the LCQDs fluorescence and a good linearity in range of 0–350 μM with a LOD of 5.34 μM. Furthermore, this sensing platform was successfully used for Fe3+ and AA monitoring in biological samples and intracellular imaging without surface modification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Fluorescence turn off/on
Real samples
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of lignin based carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and AA
Fluorescence responses for Fe3+ ions and AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of LCQDs from lignin and the Fe3+ ion and AA sensing process using LCQDs.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of LCQDs. (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of LCQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) The TEM image of LCQDs. Inset is the size distribution of LCQDs. (d) The XPS spectra of LCQDs.

(a) The PL emission spectra of LCQDs treated by different Fe3+ concentrations, Fe3+ concentration is 0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550, 600 and 650 µM (from top to bottom), respectively. The inset is the relationship between the F 0/F 1 value and Fe3+ concentrations. (b) PL responses of LCQDs in the presence of different metal ions (100 μM). F 0 and F 1 are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively.

(a) The PL responses of LCQDs/Fe3+ system towards AA at different concentrations, which is 0, 10, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350 µM (from bottom to top), respectively. Inset is the relationship between the value of (F 2/F 3 − 1) and the AA concentrations (F 2 is the PL intensity of LCQDs, F 3 is the PL intensity of LCQDs/Fe3+ in the presence of AA). (b) The PL responses of the LCQDs/Fe3+ system in the presence of different interferent (350 μM, F 4 and F 5 are the PL intensities of LCQDs/Fe3+ system in the presence and absence of different interferent, respectively).
Cell viability of HeLa cells treated with different concentrations of LCQDs.
Determination of Fe3+ and AA in human real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile and cost-effective preparation of carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid detection in living cells based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence principle

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Key Lab. of Biomass Energy and Material of Jiangsu Province, Key and Open Lab. of Forestry Chemical Engineering, State Forestry Administration, National Engineering Lab. for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Nanjing 210042, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Industry of Forestry Products
Key Lab. of Biomass Energy and Material of Jiangsu Province
Key and Open Lab. of Forestry Chemical Engineering
National Engineering Lab. for Biomass Chemical Utilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and cost-effective route of fabricating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was proposed via simply hydrothermal treatment of lignin. Because of abundant aromatic structure and functional groups derived from lignin, the prepared CQDs (LCQDs) showed unique chemical and optical property. A remarkable fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on LCQDs was found, resulting from the formation of non-fluorescence complex between LCQDs and Fe3+. This “on-off” fluorescence CQDs nanoprobe for Fe3+ allowed a wide linear range of 50–650 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 196 nM. Besides, ascorbic acid (AA) was found to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, leading to the recovery of the LCQDs fluorescence and a good linearity in range of 0–350 μM with a LOD of 5.34 μM. Furthermore, this sensing platform was successfully used for Fe3+ and AA monitoring in biological samples and intracellular imaging without surface modification.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), also called as vitamin C, is a water-soluble vitamin and the essential nutritional factor of human body [1]. AA plays a variety of crucial roles in many human physiological process, including preventing scurvy and other hemorrhagic diseases, improving immunity to prevent the common cold, promoting collagen synthesis, anti-oxidation, and so on [2]. In addition, AA is a strong reducing agent and free radical scavenger, which is also widely used in food, beverage, pharmaceutical formulations and cosmetics [3]. Low levels of AA in human body may result in atherosclerosis, cardiovascular disease, scurvy, iron deficiency anemia, and so on [4]. Therefore, a straightforward and effective analytical technique for AA detection is of great significance for the early and rapid detection of these diseases.

To date, a series of analytical strategies have been employed for AA determination in different sample matrices, including electrochemistry [5–7], chemiluminescence [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [10] and ion chromatography [11]. However, more easy but effective methods for AA detection are still in acute demand. Compared with those aforementioned detecting techniques, the fluorescent method can be carried out without sophisticated equipment or tedious pretreatment procedures [12]. The fluorescent sensors are of especial interest from the researchers due to its preponderances of versatility, selectivity, sensitivity and device portability [13,14].

In recent decade, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as an interesting class of nanomaterials after fullerene, carbon nanotubes and graphene, have been widely used in the field of material science and biomedicine because of their unique physical and chemical characteristics [15–17]. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) and traditional organic dyes, CQDs own obvious superiority, such as lower biotoxicity, size-tunable optical properties, strong photochemical (PL) stability and better biocompatibility [18–20]. On the basis of these outstanding properties, CQDs have made great progress in applications such as biological imaging, biosensing, disease diagnosis, drug delivery, catalysis and so on [21–24]. Especially as the fluorescent nanosensors, CQDs have remarkable performance in monitoring drugs, metal ions, environmental pollutants, biomolecules [15,25–27]. Although a variety of strategies have been proposed to synthesize the CQDs, seeking sustainable and low-cost carbon sources for preparing CQDs in an ecofriendly and mild way is still desirable. On the other hand, as carbon sources, biomass materials show the advantages of the CQDs fabrication due to its green chemistry nature [28]. So far, quite a lot of bio-products, such as glucose [29], chitosan [30], grass [31] and citric acid [32], and so on, were used for preparing CQDs. While most of these CQDs derived from green resources were prepared in organic solvent or with low fluorescence quantum yield (QY), which limited the application of CQDs [33]. Thus, more research is required to obtain CQDs with high optical performance based on green chemistry and economical strategies.

Lignin is an aromatic natural polymer, and its content in nature is only inferior to that of cellulose. As an excellent source of carbon, lignin exhibits amazing advances in the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials, because of high carbon content and rich aromatic structure [34]. In this work, we reported an effective sensing platform for recognition of Fe3+ and AA based on lignin derived CQDs (LCQDs, Fig. 1 ). The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups at the surface of LCQDs had high-efficiency chelation or coordination interactions with Fe3+ ion, resulting in the formation of non-fluorescence complex and an obvious PL quenching behavior of LCQDs. Meanwhile, AA was found to be able to restore the PL intensity quenched by Fe3+, due to the redox reaction between them, based on which an effective fluorescence “off-on” nanosensor was developed for AA. Furthermore, this sensing platform was successfully used for Fe3+ and AA monitoring in biological samples and intracellular imaging without surface modification.

Alkali lignin was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Trading Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaOH, BaCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2, CaCl2, KOH, NaCl, Ni(NO3)2, AlCl3, FeCl2, AgNO3, CdSO4, MgCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, PbCl2, CrCl3, Co(NO3)2, Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). l-glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), glucose (Glc), citric acid (CA), uric acid (UA), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC), l-glutathione (GSH), dopamine (DA), AA were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were made using deionized water.

The UV–vis spectra were recorded on an UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (UV-1800pc) at 800–200 nm. The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes were acquired by a FM-4P-TCSPC fluorescence spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by a PHI 500 VersaProbe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (UlVAC-PHI, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were recorded on an iS10 FTIR spectrometer at 500–5000 cm−1 (Nicolet, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a D8 Focus Power X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potential was recorded on a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, U.K.). The fluorescence (FL) spectra were measured by an LS SS Perkin Elmer spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) with the slit widths of 4 nm for excitation and emission. All the fluorescence spectra were recorded from 330 nm to 700 nm with a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. The fluorescence QY of LCQDs was estimated by the equation [25]:
Q
Y L
= Q
Y R
I L
×
A R
/
I R
×
A L
n L
/
n R
2
where the subscripts L refers to LCQDs and R is the reference, QY is the quantum yield (Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was chosen as reference, the literature reported QY of which is 54% at 360 nm excitation), I refers to the integrated intensity of the emission, n represents the refractive index of the medium, A is the absorbance at corresponding excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lignin based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In the typical process, LCQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of alkali lignin in a strong acid environment. In brief, alkali lignin (1.3 g) was added into 30 mL mixed acid (v/v, H2SO4:HNO3 = 3:1). Then the obtained solution was moved into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL) after ultrasound 2 h and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the product was neutral to pH = 7. The insoluble substances was removed by centrifugation, and the solution reacted 2 h with NaBH4 (1.3g), followed by neutralizing the raw product to pH = 7. Finally, the product was further dialyzed for 2 days and freeze-dried to obtain sample powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of LCQDs in phosphate-buffered saline solution (pH 7.4) was 0.125 mg/mL. The sensitivity test was confirmed by adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–800 μM) to 2 mL LCQDs solution and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. To study the selectivity of the prepared LCQDs, the aqueous solutions containing several kinds of metal ions (Zn2+, Na+, Al3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ba2+, K+) were prepared in a concentration of 0.01 mol/L, respectively. The fluorescence spectrums were estimated after mixing each 20 μL of the above ion solution with 2 mL LCQDs solution.

The sensitivity experiment of AA was performed by the addition of different concentrations of AA solution into the mixture of LCQDs and Fe3+ solution (the final concentration of LCQDs and Fe3+ were 0.125 mg/mL and 800 μM, respectively). To study the selectivity of LCQDs/Fe3+ system towards AA, several interferent like DA, GSH, UA, DOPAC, Glc, Gly, CA, Glu with a final concentration of 350 μM were added to the mixed solution. All FL spectra were recorded at room temperature.

To explore the practicality of the sensing system for Fe3+ and AA monitoring in complex real samples, the analysis assays were performed in the human urine and serum samples from healthy laboratory volunteers. Different amounts of Fe3+ and AA were injected into the 100-fold dilution samples, respectively, which were mixed with the sensing platform and the FL spectra were recorded.

HeLa cells were used for cytotoxicity assays and cellular imaging analysis of LCQDs. And the cytotoxicity assessment was performed by MTT assay. The above experimental processes were carried out according to our published works earlier. More details can be found in Electronic Supplementary Material.

The optical performance of LCQDs was investigated by UV–vis and PL spectra. From Fig. 2 a, it was found that there were two obvious absorption peaks located at 234 and 297 nm. The former confirms the existence of multiple polyaromatic chromophores originated from lignin. And the other peak is attributed to the aromatic π systems and oxygen-containing groups [28]. Furthermore, the inset is a comparison of the PL behavior of the sample under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right), and a bright fluorescence was recorded when the product was taken under 365 nm UV lamp. Notably, the QY of as-prepared LCQDs was obtained in the aqueous solution to be 17.3% (quinine sulfate as the standard), which exceeds more than that of pristine CQDs derived from carbon-based materials without doping [35]. The PL spectra showed a highest excitation peak at 320 nm, under which the maximum emission wavelength around 430 nm was given (Fig. 2a). Besides, the PL emission spectra of resultant LCQDs under various excitation wavelengths from 220 nm to 420 nm were obtained. As shown in Fig. 2b, the PL intensity enhanced with the increasing of excitation wavelengths from 220 nm to 320 nm, and then gradually weakened as the excitation wavelengths increase to 420 nm. Meanwhile, the position of the maximum emission peaks remained unchanged, which suggests the excitation-independent PL property of LCQDs.

The morphology of LCDs was measured by TEM (Fig. 2c), under which the LCQDs exhibited well-dispersed spherical morphology with an average diameter of 3.17 ± 0.23 nm (the inset of Fig. 2c). The XRD patterns of LCQDs (Fig. S1a) showed a broad reflection peak at 21.3°, demonstrating an amorphous structure [34]. FT-IR (Fig. S1b) spectra were used for identifying the chemical composition of LCQDs. As similar with most of published CQDs, the wide peak at 3354 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration mode of OH groups [36]. The characteristic absorption bands of CC and CO can be observed at 1575 and 1698 cm−1, respectively, and the peak at 1421 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of the carboxyl groups [37]. Moreover, other three sharp peaks at 902, 1338 and 1134 cm−1, corresponding to typical absorption bands of aromatic Ar CH, CO and COC bonds, respectively, indicate the presence of large amounts of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of LCQDs [28]. Above results were further confirmed by XPS spectra (Fig. 2d), which revealed two typical peaks at 284.9 and 531.7 eV, corresponding to the elements carbon (79.13%) and oxygen (20.87%), respectively. More information can be seen in the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra (Fig. S3). The C1s spectra (Fig. S2a) displayed three characteristic peaks, which were associated with CC/CC bonds at 284.8 eV, CO bonds at 285.4 eV and CO bonds at 288.4 eV [36]. The O1s spectra (Fig. S2b) were divided into three types of peaks that assigned to OCO/ArOAr (531 eV), CO/COC (531.5 eV) and COH (532.7 eV) [38]. Above analysis results indicate the presence of abundant hydrophilic functional groups, which is helpful for improving the water solubility and stability of LCQDs in aqueous samples. On the other hand, the existence of these oxygen-containing functional groups brings in plenty of surface defects, thus leading to the outstanding PL performance of LCQDs [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses for Fe3+ ions and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Previous works have demonstrated the excellent binding affinity of oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl groups, towards Fe3+ [40]. Herein, the prepared LCQDs are expected to be a fluorescent nanosensor for Fe3+ determination, because of the numerous oxygen-containing groups derived from lignin. As we all know, Fe3+, as one of the essential elements in human body, plays an irreplaceable role in transport function and hematopoietic in the human blood [41]. The disorders of Fe3+ in human body may lead to some diseases, including metabolic abnormalities, cell anemia, tissue damage, and even organ failure [42]. Although a great deal of analytical methods for Fe3+ have been successfully developed, including atomic absorption spectrometry [43], chromatography [44], and electrochemical [45], etc., efforts continue the search for even more simple, effective and sensitive approaches for the determination of Fe3+.

Firstly, the effect of pH value on the PL performance of LCQDs solution was studied. As shown in Fig. S3a, the PL intensity exhibited pH dependent and reached the maximum in the range of 8–10. Furthermore, the PL responses of LCQDs towards Fe3+ at different pH value were explored. From Fig. S3b, an obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon was found as the addition of Fe3+ to LCQDs solution. An enhancement of pH value from 1.0 to 7.0 resulted in the increasing of fluorescence quenching efficiency (1 − F 1/F 0, F 1 and F 0 are the PL intensities of LCQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively), which achieved maximum in the neutral condition (pH = 7.0). And a further increasing of pH value to 10.0 led to the quenching efficiency reducing. Fe3+ is existence in hydroxide in the alkaline solution, which probably goes against the accurate detection of Fe3+. Thus, the pH of 7.0 was selected for the system.

Moreover, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the PL intensity of LCQDs reduced gradually with the increasing contents of Fe3+. The linear response (the inset of Fig. 3a) of LCQDs towards Fe3+ was observed in a range of 50–650 μM with a calibration equation of F 0/F 1 = 0.008CFe
3
+ + 1.2838 (CFe
3
+ is the concentration of Fe3+, F 0 and F 1 are the PL intensities of LCQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively). A good linear correlation (R 2 = 0.994) was obtained with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 196 nM (S/N = 3). Table S1 shows the achievements on working range and LOD of some different methods for Fe3+ detection. Compared with these results, the proposed sensing platform shows more superior performance.

To further evaluate the selectivity of LCQDs for Fe3+, the fluorescence response tests (Fig. 3b) of other various ions (Zn2+, Na+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ba2+, K+) were carried out under the same condition. As was quite obvious, the addition of Fe3+ led to noticeable fluorescence quenching, and the others almost had little effects on the fluorescence behavior of LCQDs. This result proves the selectivity of LCQDs as a “turn-off” fluorescent system for Fe3+. Besides, note that, among above ions Fe2+ also did not cause significant changes in PL intensity. As is known that Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ in the presence of AA, inspired by which a “turn-on” chemosensor for AA detection could be built. It is on account of this fact, the PL quenched LCQDs/Fe3+ probe was restored with the addition of AA. Therefore, the LCQDs/Fe3+ can be used as a fluorescent “turn-on” sensing platform for AA detection.

According to the above strategy, the sensitivity and selectivity of LCQDs/Fe3+ sensing system was evaluated. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, the PL intensity of LCQDs/Fe3+ system was gradually strengthened by the increasing AA concentration. And a significant linearity relationship of F 2/F 3 (F 2 is the PL intensity of LCQDs, F 3 is the PL intensity of LCQDs/Fe3+ in the presence of AA) against the AA concentration (0–350 μM) was observed, which can be curve-fitted into F 2/F 3 = 6.85–0.0143CAA (CAA is the concentration of AA, R 2 = 0.992). The LOD for AA is 5.34 μM according to the signal-to-noise of 3. These results are comparable with or better than those of published fluorescent monitor for AA, as shown in Table S1. Meanwhile, the selectivity tests (Fig. 4b) of LCQDs/Fe3+ sensing platform for AA were performed in the presence of representative interferent including DA, GSH, UA, Dopac, Glc, Gly, Glu and CA at same condition. Such common biological molecules as DA, GSH, UA, Dopac, Glc, Gly, Glu and CA have negligible effect on the fluorescent emission of LCQDs/Fe3+. Compared with these results, the most notable fluorescence alterations of LCQDs/Fe3+ were observed after the addition of AA, proving the good selectivity of this sensing platform based on “turn-on” fluorescent principle for AA detection.

The XPS and FT-IR proved the existence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups with negative charge, which have strong affinity with Fe3+. In this work, the remarkable fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ ion on the prepared LCQDs results from the high-efficiency chelation or coordination interactions between them [46]. During this course, part of electrons/charges in the excited LCQDs was induced to pass on the half-filled 3d of Fe3+, leading to the PL quenching [42]. The variation of zeta potential value of LCQDs from −12.83 to 8.12 mV after the addition of Fe3+, further confirms the coordination effect between the organic functional groups on the surface of LCQDS and Fe3+ ion [40]. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime tests of LCQDs and LCQDs/Fe3+ were performed. From Fig. S4, it was found an ultrafast exciton recombination phenomenon attributed to the lifetime of LCQDs as low as 6.11 ns. And after chelation with Fe3+, the average life time was changed to 6.07 ns, which indicates a static quenching arising from the formation of a quite stable non-fluorescence complex between LCQDs and Fe3+ [47]. Whereafter, with the addition of AA, the PL intensity of LCQDs was recovered, which is likely due to the redox reaction between LCQDs/Fe3+ and AA. For LCQDs/Fe3+ system, Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by AA, resulting in minimization of the nonradiative electron transfer and the PL restoration of LCQDs [42].

In order to evaluate the practicality of this method, the prepared LCQDs were applied for detecting Fe3+ in human serum and urine using standard addition method. Different quantity of Fe3+ was added into a 100-fold dilution serum and urine samples before analysis. As listed in Table 1 , the good recoveries of the sensing platform at each Fe3+ concentrations were found in a range of 95.5–107.1%, with a low relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 6.5%. These consequences suggest remarkable practicability of this sensor for Fe3+ monitoring in biological samples. Additionally, the same measurements were performed with the LCQDs/Fe3+ system in serum and urine samples. The obtained results summarized in Table 1, it was found that recovery values of this strategy were within the range of 92.3–108.5% with the RSD of less than 7.2%, revealed the excellent capable of detecting AA in human real sample. Above results demonstrate that the proposed sensing platform displays the reliability and feasibility for Fe3+ and AA determination in the human biological samples.

Before cellular imaging and clinical applications, it is necessary to explore the cytotoxicity of LCQDs. For the cytotoxicity experiments, the viability of HeLa cells treated with LCQDs was determined by a standard MTT assay. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the cell viability was not significantly harmed by the increasing concentrations of LCQDs. Even the concentration of LCQDs reached 1.0 mg/mL, the cell viability still remain 90% after cultured for 24 h, suggesting instinctively low toxicity of the LCQDs.

The cellular imaging performance of LCQDs was researched. As shown in Fig. S5, a strong blue PL emission (Fig. S5a) from the intracellular area could be found by fluorescence microscope after treating Hela cells with LCQDs for 6 h at 37 °C. As expected, after adding Fe3+ ions, intracellular PL emission weakened substantially (Fig. S5b) and recovered by introducing AA (Fig. S5c), which revealed that the prepared nanoprobe had great potential as an effective tool for intracellular Fe3+ and AA determination based on PL turn off-on strategy.

In summary, a kind of carbon quantum dots with high fluorescence property has been prepared by a green, simple, easy-to-operate route from natural aromatic carbon source. This lignin derived LCQDs was successfully utilized as a highly sensitive and selective chemosensor for Fe3+ and AA determination based on fluorescence off-on switch strategy. More significantly, this nanosensor can also be used in human real samples for Fe3+ and AA monitoring with excellent recovery. It had low toxicity and offered excellent performance in cellular imaging and detection. Therefore, the proposed method would open the avenue of detection and diagnosis of intracellular Fe3+ and AA levels in biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Fundamental Research Funds of Research Institute of Forest New Technology, CAF (CAFYBB2017SY033).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.11.095.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near infrared graphene quantum dots-based two-photon nanoprobe for direct bioimaging of endogenous ascorbic acid in living cells
Novel graphene flowers modified carbon fibers for simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid

An electrochemical sensor based on reduced graphene oxide decorated with polypyrrole nanofibers and zinc oxide–copper oxide p–n junction heterostructures for the simultaneous voltammetric determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine, paracetamol, and tryptophan
Label-free simultaneous analysis of Fe(III) and ascorbic acid using fluorescence switching of ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
Electroanalysis moves towards paper-based printed electronics: carbon black nanomodified inkjet-printed sensor for ascorbic acid detection as a case study
Portable electrochemical sensor based on 4-aminobenzoic acid-functionalized herringbone carbon nanotubes for the determination of ascorbic acid and uric acid in human fluids
Bimetallic-organic framework derived porous Co3O4/Fe3O4/C-loaded g-C3N4 nanocomposites as non-enzymic electrocatalysis oxidization toward ascorbic acid, dopamine acid, and uric acid
Bimodal electrochemiluminescence of G-CNQDs in the presence of double coreactants for ascorbic acid detection
A quick method for the simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid and sorbic acid in fruit juices by capillary zone electrophoresis
Determination of ascorbic acid and carotenoids in food commodities by liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry detection
Determination of ascorbic acid by polypyrrole potentiometric detector in ion chromatography
Size-dependent modulation of fluorescence and light scattering: a new strategy for development of ratiometric sensing
Controllable and eco-friendly synthesis of P-riched carbon quantum dots and its application for copper (II) ion sensing
A dual-model strategy for fluorometric determination of ascorbic acid and of ascorbic acid oxidase activity by using DNA-templated gold-silver nanoclusters
Design principles of chiral carbon nanodots help convey chirality from molecular to nanoscale level
A simple route to porous graphene from carbon nanodots for supercapacitor applications
Disentangling size effects and spectral inhomogeneity in carbon nanodots by ultrafast dynamical hole-burning
Novel thermal quenching characteristics of luminescent carbon nanodots via tailoring the surface chemical groups
Customizing the electrochemical properties of carbon nanodots by using quinones in bottom-up synthesis
Artifacts and errors associated with the ubiquitous presence of fluorescent impurities in carbon nanodots
Spore-derived color-tunable multi-doped carbon nanodots as sensitive nanosensors and intracellular imaging agents

Carbonization of human fingernails: toward the sustainable production of multifunctional nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon nanodots with highly luminescent probing and cell proliferative/migration properties
NiFe LDH nanodots anchored on 3D macro/mesoporous carbon as a high-performance ORR/OER bifunctional electrocatalyst
One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples
Mechanofluorochromic carbon nanodots: controllable pressure-triggered blue- and red-shifted photoluminescence
Carbon nanodots based biosensors for gene mutation detection
Lignin derived reduced fluorescence carbon dots with theranostic approaches: nano-drug-carrier and bioimaging
Structure and solvents effects on the optical properties of sugar-derived carbon nanodots
